does: This paper examines the instrumental convergence thesis—the claim that diverse AI systems will converge on pursuing power as an instrumental goal regardless of their final objectives. The author analyzes arguments that power-seeking is an inevitable consequence of having any goal in a sufficiently complex environment, and critically evaluates leading defenses of this thesis. Through careful philosophical analysis, the paper argues that existing defenses of instrumental convergence fail to establish the thesis in the strong form required to support claims that advanced AI systems will necessarily seek power and thereby pose existential risks to humanity. The work provides a systematic examination of the logical structure and assumptions underlying power-seeking arguments in AI safety.
connects: This work engages directly with foundational literature in AI safety and existential risk that has posited power-seeking as a central concern, building on prior analyses of instrumental goals and convergent behavior in artificial agents. The paper responds to and extends discussions in longtermism and AI governance that have relied on instrumental convergence arguments, particularly work suggesting that sufficiently capable AI systems will naturally pursue power acquisition as a means to achieving arbitrary final goals. It connects to broader philosophical work on rational agency, goal-directedness, and the relationship between means and ends, while also engaging with specific technical and conceptual work on AI safety assumptions and their empirical or logical foundations.
enables: By critically examining the foundations of power-seeking arguments, this work opens space for more nuanced discussions about which AI risks are robustly grounded versus those depending on controversial assumptions. The analysis enables researchers to identify which aspects of the instrumental convergence thesis require stronger justification and points toward more targeted investigations into specific conditions under which power-seeking might actually emerge. The paper suggests implications for how AI governance and safety research should be structured and what priorities deserve emphasis if instrumental convergence cannot be assumed. It also indicates the importance of developing alternative frameworks for thinking about AI risks that do not depend on inevitable power-seeking, potentially reorienting longtermism debates toward more empirically tractable and theoretically defensible concerns.
